intro: AI infrastructure and geopolitics dominate today's news, with South Korea making a historic $1T bet on chips and humanoids, OpenAI previewing its next-gen model family, and a landmark Supreme Court ruling reshaping digital privacy law.

stories.title: OpenAI previews GPT-5.6 series: Sol, Terra, and Luna
stories.summary: OpenAI has begun a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 model family, which comes in three tiers: Sol (flagship), Terra (balanced, everyday use), and Luna (fast and cheap). Sol is positioned as OpenAI's most capable model yet, with particular strength in coding, science, and cybersecurity tasks, and comes paired with what OpenAI calls its most advanced safety stack. Terra is notably positioned as offering performance competitive with GPT-5.5 at half the price — that's a meaningful cost reduction for developers building on the API. Luna rounds out the family for latency-sensitive, cost-constrained applications. OpenAI says broad general availability is coming in the coming weeks, which means this preview phase is short. The naming convention — celestial bodies — is a departure from the numbered conventions, possibly signaling a branding shift as the model families multiply. The cybersecurity angle is interesting: OpenAI is leaning hard into enterprise security use cases with its Daybreak initiative, and Sol appears purpose-built to support that. For developers, the key number to watch is Terra's 2x cost reduction over GPT-5.5 — if the quality holds up under real workloads, that changes budget math substantially. This announcement also reinforces that OpenAI is now releasing models in rapid succession, compressing the window between generations. Worth keeping an eye on benchmark comparisons against Anthropic's Claude and Google's Gemini 3.5 series when Sol goes GA.

stories.title: Gemini's personalized AI image generation opens to free US users
stories.summary: Google has expanded Gemini's personalized image generation feature to free users in the United States, allowing the chatbot to create images tailored to individual interests and data pulled from connected Google apps. Previously, this feature was gated behind Gemini Advanced (the paid tier), so opening it to free users is a meaningful distribution move. The personalization aspect is the interesting wrinkle here — rather than generic image generation, Gemini can now draw on what it knows about you from Gmail, Google Photos, Search history, and other connected services to produce more contextually relevant results. That's either a compelling capability or a privacy concern depending on your perspective, and Google is betting users will see it as the former. This is part of Google's broader strategy of deeply integrating Gemini across its product ecosystem, which is arguably Google's strongest differentiator against standalone AI products. The move also signals that Google is comfortable enough with the quality and safety of personalized generation to take it out of the paid tier. For competitors like Adobe Firefly or Midjourney, having Google give this away for free to billions of users is real competitive pressure. It also raises questions about how Google is handling data governance when AI systems start actively synthesizing outputs from personal data across multiple services. The feature is US-only for now, which likely reflects regulatory caution in other markets, particularly Europe.

stories.title: Ornith-1.0: New open-weights model for agentic coding hits strong benchmarks
stories.summary: DeepReinforce has released Ornith-1.0, an open-weights (MIT licensed) model family specifically designed for agentic coding tasks, with variants ranging from 9B Dense up to a 397B MoE. The models are built on top of pretrained Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 base models, which DeepReinforce has then fine-tuned using reinforcement learning techniques oriented toward self-scaffolding — meaning the model can better plan and execute multi-step coding tasks autonomously. The self-scaffolding framing is notable: rather than just autocompleting code, the model is designed to handle the kind of iterative, tool-using workflows that agentic coding environments like Cursor require. It reportedly achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models of comparable size on coding benchmarks, which is a meaningful claim given how crowded that space has gotten. The MIT license is a significant choice — it means developers and companies can use, modify, and deploy these models without restrictions, which distinguishes it from models with more restrictive licenses. The 35B MoE variant is likely the sweet spot for teams running local inference, offering a balance of capability and hardware requirements. This is DeepReinforce's first public model release, so they're entering with a bang rather than a modest debut. The timing aligns with surging developer interest in agentic coding tools — Cursor launched a mobile app this week, Base44 launched its own model, and Chamath Palihapitiya just raised $135M for an AI coding startup. For anyone building on or evaluating coding models, Ornith-1.0 deserves a close look given the permissive licensing and strong initial benchmark claims.

stories.title: New report finds AI-heavy companies actually grew headcount, including junior roles
stories.summary: A new report has complicated the dominant narrative around AI and job displacement, finding that companies classified as 'high-intensity AI adopters' saw overall headcount grow by 10.2%, with entry-level headcount specifically rising 12%. This directly contradicts the widely circulated claim that AI is killing junior jobs — at least in aggregate, and at least among companies that are deeply integrating AI into their workflows. The counterintuitive explanation likely involves a few dynamics: AI tools increase productivity, which can expand the scope of what teams take on, which in turn creates demand for more workers rather than fewer. It's also possible that AI adoption is concentrated in growing industries and companies, which would inflate the numbers. The report doesn't appear to settle the question definitively — correlation between AI adoption and headcount growth doesn't mean AI is causing the growth. That said, for the 'AI will eliminate all the junior devs' crowd, this is a meaningful data point to reckon with. From a practical standpoint, this matters for how companies think about hiring, training, and workforce planning in an AI-integrated environment. It also matters for policy discussions about retraining programs and AI regulation. The findings from this report sit in interesting tension with anecdotal evidence from tech companies doing layoffs while citing AI efficiency gains — the aggregate picture may be masking significant variation by sector and company size.

stories.title: Anthropic cuts California government a 50% deal on Claude
stories.summary: Anthropic has struck a deal with California Governor Gavin Newsom to provide the state government access to its Claude AI models at half the standard price. This is notable both commercially and politically: it deepens Anthropic's relationship with California at a moment when the company's relationship with the federal government is reportedly adversarial. The deal gives California agencies access to enterprise-grade AI tooling at a price point that makes broad deployment feasible across government services. For Anthropic, it's a reference customer with enormous scale — California's government is one of the largest employers in the US — and a strategic hedge given federal headwinds. The timing is interesting: as AI regulation debates play out at both state and federal levels, having Anthropic embedded in California's government creates a natural alignment of interests. Newsom has positioned California as the counterweight to federal AI policy, so this partnership signals where Anthropic is placing some of its political bets. From a competitive standpoint, this is the kind of deal OpenAI and Google would very much like to win, and losing California to Anthropic stings. It also suggests government procurement is becoming a real battleground for frontier AI labs — and that pricing flexibility is a key differentiator. The article notes the federal government has made an 'enemy' of Anthropic, which is a strong framing that deserves more context, but suggests the company is navigating a genuinely complicated political landscape.

stories.title: OpenAI and Broadcom's Jalapeño: a custom LLM inference chip
stories.summary: OpenAI and Broadcom have unveiled a custom AI chip called Jalapeño, designed specifically for LLM inference workloads. This is a significant strategic move for OpenAI: rather than being entirely dependent on Nvidia's GPU supply chain, building custom inference silicon gives OpenAI more control over cost, performance, and capacity planning. Inference — running trained models to generate responses — is where the bulk of OpenAI's compute costs live at production scale, so optimizing that layer is directly tied to margins and pricing competitiveness. Broadcom is an interesting choice of partner; they have deep experience in custom ASIC design and have worked with Google on the TPU family, so they bring real silicon expertise. Custom inference chips can be optimized for specific model architectures and attention patterns in ways that general-purpose GPUs can't match, potentially yielding significant efficiency gains. This follows a broader trend of AI hyperscalers building custom silicon: Google has TPUs, Amazon has Trainium and Inferentia, Microsoft is developing Maia — OpenAI was somewhat late to this game. The chip's name, Jalapeño, continues OpenAI's recent trend of naming things with a bit of personality. Details on performance specs and when this will be deployed at scale haven't been fully disclosed yet. The move also signals that OpenAI sees its future as a vertically integrated AI infrastructure company, not just a model provider sitting on top of third-party cloud compute.

stories.title: MIT Tech Review: AI agents aren't your coworkers, and framing matters
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review has published a sharp piece pushing back on the trend of AI companies giving their agents human names and presenting them as team members or 'coworkers.' The piece argues that this framing is both misleading and consequential — not just as marketing fluff, but as a design choice that shapes how humans interact with and delegate to AI systems. When a company introduces 'Alex' the AI agent rather than 'an AI tool that does X,' it changes the psychological contract workers have with the system and potentially obscures accountability. The author draws a distinction between AI-as-tool and AI-as-colleague that has real implications for how errors get caught, how oversight gets exercised, and who gets blamed when things go wrong. This connects to a broader debate in the industry about agent anthropomorphization, which has been growing as agentic AI systems take on longer-horizon tasks with less human supervision. There's a practical engineering angle here too: if you treat your AI agent like a capable colleague you trust, you're less likely to implement the verification and audit loops that catch failure modes. The piece is a useful counterweight to the wave of 'AI coworker' marketing coming out of enterprise software vendors. It's worth reading alongside the OpenAI research on how agents are transforming work, which takes a more optimistic framing. The tension between these two perspectives — AI as transformative colleague vs. AI as powerful but limited tool — is one of the defining debates of the current moment in AI deployment.

stories.title: South Korea commits $1T to memory chips and humanoid robots — 'RAMageddon' response takes shape
stories.summary: South Korea has announced a staggering $1 trillion investment in memory chip production and humanoid robotics, with Samsung and SK Hynix committing over $550 billion specifically to address the global memory shortage that's been dubbed 'RAMageddon' in some quarters. The chip investment is a direct response to AI infrastructure demand — training and running large models requires enormous amounts of high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and supply constraints have been a real bottleneck. South Korea's two largest memory makers are essentially betting that AI-driven memory demand will sustain massive capacity expansion for years to come. The humanoid robotics component targets commercial deployment by 2028, positioning South Korea to compete with US and Chinese efforts in physical AI — a term being used to describe AI systems that operate in the real world through robotic bodies. The $1 trillion figure is remarkable for its scale: it represents a national industrial policy bet comparable to the US CHIPS Act but concentrated in a single country's private sector with government backing. For the global AI supply chain, this matters because HBM supply from Korean manufacturers (primarily SK Hynix, which currently dominates HBM for Nvidia GPUs) has been a constraint on how quickly AI infrastructure can scale. More fab capacity means potentially easing that constraint over the next few years. The humanoid angle is less immediately impactful but signals where South Korea sees the next competitive frontier. This is a country that knows what happens when you're late to a technology transition — it didn't want to miss the AI hardware wave.

stories.title: IBM unveils sub-1nm chip technology that could extend Moore's Law a decade
stories.summary: IBM has revealed a prototype chip containing around 100 billion transistors on an area roughly the size of a fingernail, achieving transistor density that breaks the 1-nanometer barrier — a milestone many thought would require entirely new computing paradigms to reach. This is approximately twice the density of IBM's own previous state-of-the-art chip announced in 2021, representing a significant leap in a relatively short time. The technology, which IBM describes as using a novel 'block of flats' vertical stacking architecture, could pave the way for substantially faster and more energy-efficient processors across a range of applications. The key word here is 'prototype' — IBM is not claiming this is ready for production, and the path from lab demonstration to fab-ready process node typically takes years and involves massive engineering challenges. That said, IBM Research has a strong track record of roadmapping future chip generations, and their announcements in this space tend to be technically credible even when timelines slip. The energy efficiency angle may actually be more important than raw performance for AI applications: running large inference workloads at scale is enormously power-hungry, and chips that deliver more compute per watt directly reduce operating costs and environmental impact. For the broader semiconductor industry, this is a shot across the bow at TSMC and Intel, both of which are working on their own sub-2nm process nodes. If IBM's techniques are licensable or applicable to logic chips (not just their own products), the implications extend well beyond IBM's own hardware lineup.

stories.title: Rocket Lab acquires Iridium in historic deal
stories.summary: Rocket Lab has announced the acquisition of Iridium, the satellite communications company that operates a constellation of 66 low-earth orbit satellites providing global voice and data coverage. This is a genuinely historic deal that transforms Rocket Lab from a launch services company into a vertically integrated space communications business in one move. Iridium is not a small asset: it's the only commercial satellite network with true pole-to-pole global coverage, and it's used by everyone from maritime shipping to aviation to military and emergency services. For Rocket Lab, the acquisition provides an immediate revenue base from Iridium's subscription business, which generates hundreds of millions in annual revenue — far more stable than launch contracts. It also gives Rocket Lab a captive customer for future launches as Iridium's constellation eventually needs replenishment or upgrades. The strategic logic mirrors what SpaceX did by building Starlink alongside its launch business — having an orbital infrastructure business reduces dependence on winning launch contracts from competitors. Iridium's technology is older than Starlink's and operates on a different frequency band (L-band), serving a somewhat different market: reliability and global coverage matter more than high bandwidth for Iridium's core customers. The deal raises interesting questions about Rocket Lab's ambitions — are they positioning themselves as an end-to-end space infrastructure company, or is this primarily a financial move to stabilize revenues? Either way, this significantly changes the competitive landscape for space communications.

stories.title: Supreme Court ruling significantly limits geofence warrants — with EU-US data transfer implications
stories.summary: The US Supreme Court has issued a major ruling substantially restricting the government's use of geofence warrants — the controversial law enforcement tool that allows police to request data from Google and other tech companies about all devices present in a geographic area during a specific time window. The Court stopped short of declaring geofence warrants categorically unconstitutional, but established significant Fourth Amendment constraints on when and how they can be used, effectively gutting their utility as a broad investigative dragnet. This has been a significant civil liberties battleground for several years: geofence warrants have been used to identify suspects at crime scenes but have also swept up innocent bystanders who simply happened to be nearby. The ruling is a win for digital privacy advocates who argued these warrants are fundamentally incompatible with constitutional protections against unreasonable search. From a tech industry perspective, this reduces the burden on companies like Google, which has received thousands of geofence warrants and had to develop internal processes to respond to them. The EU angle is significant: privacy advocacy group noyb has argued this ruling also disrupts the legal framework underpinning EU-US data transfers, since US government surveillance authorities are a key concern under European data protection law. If US courts are now acknowledging meaningful limits on government access to location data, that could actually strengthen the legal foundation for data transfers — or it could create new questions about which data is now more or less protected. The ruling is being analyzed across multiple dimensions simultaneously and will have downstream effects on law enforcement practice, tech company compliance obligations, and international data governance.

stories.title: Google warns EU data-sharing proposals create serious security risks
stories.summary: Google has gone public with warnings that the European Union's proposed antitrust remedies — which would require Google to share its search index data with competitors and open up Android's AI features — create serious privacy and security vulnerabilities. The warnings came from senior Google security staff who argue that sharing search data at the scale and granularity the EU is requesting would create a high-value target that could be compromised, exposing user query histories and behavioral data to potential attackers. The Android component of the EU's demands would require Google to open up the AI systems built into Android to third-party integration, which Google says creates similar risks around system-level access. This is a classic tech company playbook move: when faced with regulatory requirements to open up platforms, invoke security and privacy concerns to slow or soften the remedies. That doesn't mean the concerns are entirely fabricated — there are real security engineering challenges in safely sharing data with competitors — but the framing conveniently aligns with Google's competitive interests. The EU's Digital Markets Act (DMA) framework is pushing hard on search and mobile OS as gatekeeper markets, and Google has been fighting these battles in multiple jurisdictions simultaneously. For developers and the broader tech ecosystem, forced data sharing with search index access could actually lower barriers to entry for alternative search engines, which have historically struggled to compete with Google partly because of the data moat. The EU will likely push back on Google's framing and demand technical specifics about what security measures would make compliance possible.

stories.title: US offers $10M for info on Russian groups behind Signal and WhatsApp hacking campaign
stories.summary: The US government has announced a $10 million reward for information leading to the identification or location of individuals behind an ongoing hacking campaign targeting Signal and WhatsApp users, attributed to two Russian state-sponsored groups. The operation has reportedly been active since at least March and represents a focused effort to compromise end-to-end encrypted communications platforms that are widely used by journalists, activists, government officials, and security-conscious individuals globally. The attack methodology reportedly involves exploiting weaknesses in the platforms' account linking or device registration flows rather than breaking the encryption itself — a reminder that operational security failures around device management often matter more than the underlying cryptographic strength. This is significant because Signal in particular has long been the gold standard recommendation for secure communications, and a state-sponsored campaign specifically targeting it warrants serious attention. The $10 million bounty signals that the US government views this as a serious national security matter, not just a criminal hacking case. For regular users, the practical implication is to ensure multi-device linking is reviewed, registration lock is enabled on Signal, and any unusual account activity is investigated promptly. The timing is interesting given WhatsApp is simultaneously rolling out usernames to reduce phone number exposure — a separate privacy improvement that has nothing to do with this campaign but speaks to the broader pressure on messaging platforms to improve their security surface. Russian state groups have been increasingly aggressive in targeting secure communications infrastructure, and this campaign appears to be part of a sustained intelligence collection effort.

stories.title: Meta caught having contractors pose as teenagers to test rival chatbots on sensitive topics
stories.summary: WIRED has published an investigation revealing that hundreds of contractors working for Meta systematically posed as teenagers when interacting with competitor AI chatbots — including Google's Gemini and OpenAI's ChatGPT — specifically to probe how those systems respond to sensitive topics like suicide, sexual content, and drug use. The project appears to have been a structured competitive intelligence effort: by having contractors roleplay as minors and present high-risk scenarios, Meta could document where competing products failed their own safety guardrails. This is ethically murky territory. On one hand, red-teaming AI systems for safety vulnerabilities is a legitimate and necessary practice. On the other hand, doing it systematically against competitors' products while misrepresenting the identity of testers raises questions about the intent and about what Meta planned to do with the findings. The obvious charitable reading is that Meta was benchmarking its own safety work against competitors. The less charitable reading is that this was opposition research designed to find and potentially exploit embarrassing failure modes in rivals' products. The fact that this involved contractors pretending to be at-risk teenagers adds a layer of discomfort beyond standard competitive analysis. For AI safety researchers and regulators, this highlights a real gap: there are no established norms or rules about how companies can probe competitors' AI systems, and the line between safety research and competitive intelligence gathering is genuinely blurry. Meta has not commented extensively on the nature of the program.

stories.title: Cursor launches mobile app for remote oversight of coding agents
stories.summary: Cursor, the AI-powered code editor that has become one of the most widely adopted tools among developers using AI for coding, has launched a mobile app designed specifically for monitoring and guiding coding agents while away from a desktop. The app isn't meant for writing code on a phone — nobody wants to do that — but rather for reviewing what your coding agent is doing, approving or redirecting steps, and maintaining oversight over longer-running autonomous tasks. This reflects the shift in how AI coding tools are being used: increasingly, developers are setting up agents to work on tasks over hours or days, and the ability to check in and course-correct without sitting at a computer is genuinely useful. It's a thoughtful product decision that treats the mobile experience as a supervision interface rather than trying to replicate the full IDE experience on a small screen. The launch is well-timed given the industry's rapid move toward more autonomous coding workflows: Cursor is essentially acknowledging that agents need human oversight and building infrastructure to make that oversight more convenient rather than arguing the agents are autonomous enough to not need it. For teams already using Cursor heavily, this reduces the friction of agent-assisted development during off-hours or while traveling. The broader trend here is interesting: as AI coding tools get more capable, the product challenge shifts from 'how do we help developers write code' to 'how do we help developers manage AI systems that write code' — a meaningful reframing of the problem.

stories.title: Base44 launches its own AI model as vibe coding platforms seek differentiation
stories.summary: Base44, the vibe coding platform owned by Wix that allows users to build applications through natural language interfaces, has started rolling out its own proprietary AI model with ambitions to eventually outperform frontier models for its specific use case. This is part of a broader pattern of AI application companies deciding that relying entirely on third-party model APIs is a strategic vulnerability — when your core product is built on OpenAI or Anthropic, you're subject to their pricing, availability, and capability roadmap decisions. Base44's move to develop its own model is notable because Wix has substantial resources and a clear use case: generating functional web applications from natural language descriptions. Training a model specifically on that task, with feedback loops from real user interactions, is a plausible path to outperforming general-purpose frontier models on the specific job of code generation for web apps. The 'defensibility' framing in the headline is apt — in a world where every AI startup is built on the same underlying models, owning your model stack is one of the few genuine moats available. This week has seen similar moves from multiple directions: Chamath Palihapitiya raised $135M for an AI coding startup, Cursor launched mobile oversight tooling, and Ornith-1.0 released open-weights coding models. The AI coding tools space is simultaneously crowded and rapidly evolving, and the companies that will survive long-term are the ones building proprietary data and model advantages. Base44's bet is that vertical specialization beats general capability for their specific use case.

stories.title: .self: A new top-level domain designed for self-hosting
stories.summary: A proposal for a new top-level domain called '.self' has been gaining significant attention in developer communities, designed specifically to support self-hosting workflows. The idea, proposed by the Human-Centered Computing Foundation (HCCF), is to create a TLD that resolves to local or self-hosted infrastructure rather than external DNS — effectively giving self-hosted services a clean, memorable namespace without requiring valid external domain registration or complex local DNS configuration. For developers running home labs or self-hosted services, the current situation is awkward: you either use `.local` (with its mDNS quirks), set up your own internal DNS server, or use a real domain you've registered but pointed at internal IPs. A purpose-built '.self' TLD that browsers and operating systems understood as self-hosted could significantly lower the barrier to setting up and maintaining personal infrastructure. The Hacker News community has given this strong attention (470 points), suggesting real developer appetite for a cleaner self-hosting story. There are obvious technical challenges: getting browser vendors, OS vendors, and ICANN to agree on a new reserved TLD is not a small undertaking. The proposal would need to specify clearly that '.self' domains never resolve over public DNS, similar to how '.localhost' and '.local' work. This sits in an interesting moment when self-hosting is having a genuine revival, partly driven by concerns about cloud service reliability (see: Sony deleting purchased content this week), privacy, and cost.

stories.title: Sony erasing digital content from libraries is a reminder you don't own what you 'buy'
stories.summary: Sony has continued scaling down its digital content store, with recent moves resulting in purchased content being removed from users' libraries — content they paid for and reasonably expected to keep. This isn't a new story, but it keeps happening with enough regularity that it deserves continued attention: digital purchases are not ownership in any meaningful traditional sense, they are licenses that can be revoked. Sony has been gradually winding down its digital storefront for several years, and each phase of that wind-down results in some customers losing access to media they paid for. The legal situation is clear and depressing: terms of service for virtually all digital content purchases explicitly state that you're buying a license, not a copy, and the licensor can terminate that license. Consumers generally don't read these terms, and the entire UX of digital storefronts is designed to feel like purchase rather than rental. The broader context includes a wave of similar situations: Microsoft closing its ebook store, streaming services removing content that's still nominally available to purchase, and gaming platforms delisting titles. The Kobo/StoryGraph partnership announced this week (allowing reading data to sync away from Amazon's Goodreads) is almost an interesting counterpoint — at least some companies are thinking about data portability and ecosystem independence. The '.self' TLD story and this Sony story are in some ways two sides of the same coin: as cloud services prove unreliable custodians of your data and purchases, self-hosting and local ownership are looking more attractive.

stories.title: Waymo and Uber quietly end their Phoenix partnership
stories.summary: Waymo and Uber have parted ways in Phoenix, ending a partnership that had seen Waymo robotaxis available through the Uber app in the city. Uber confirmed it is preparing to launch a different autonomous vehicle partnership in Phoenix but declined to name the new partner, which immediately raises questions about who's stepping in — the leading candidates being Cruise (if it's operational again), Zoox, or one of the newer AV players. The Waymo-Uber relationship has always been complicated by history: Uber was sued by Waymo's parent Alphabet in 2017 for alleged trade secret theft related to lidar technology, a case that settled for $245 million in Uber equity. They later patched things up commercially, but the split suggests the partnership dynamics were never fully comfortable. For Waymo, losing the Uber distribution channel in Phoenix matters less than it once might have — Waymo One has been steadily building its own direct-to-consumer presence and is operational in multiple cities. For Uber, the calculus is about ensuring it has AV supply regardless of which technology wins, and not being overly dependent on any single AV provider. The unnamed new partner is the key mystery here: if Uber has found an AV company willing to give it favorable terms, that could reshape the competitive dynamics for robotaxi deployment. Phoenix has been one of the most important AV testing and deployment markets given its wide roads and generally favorable weather, making any shift in partnerships there worth watching.

stories.title: One million passports leaked online in cannabis club data breach
stories.summary: A significant data breach has resulted in approximately one million passport documents being leaked online, traced back to a security failure at cannabis club management systems provider Nefos, which operated under the product name PuffPal. This is a particularly sensitive category of breach: passport data includes document numbers, issue and expiry dates, and often photographs and personal details that can be used for identity fraud, account takeovers, and in some cases travel document forgery. Cannabis clubs in jurisdictions where recreational use is legal frequently collect and store government ID as part of their compliance obligations for age and identity verification — making them an unlikely but real custodian of sensitive identity documents at scale. The Nefos/PuffPal breach illustrates a persistent problem in the compliance-tech space: companies that serve highly regulated industries often collect sensitive data to satisfy regulatory requirements but don't invest in security infrastructure commensurate with the sensitivity of what they're holding. A million passports is a large enough dataset to be genuinely dangerous in the hands of organized fraud operations. The breach also raises questions about data retention practices — why was this data stored in a form that could be leaked rather than processed and discarded? For anyone who has visited a cannabis club that used digital ID verification in the past few years, checking whether your documents were involved is advisable.

stories.title: US renewable energy passes key milestone: renewables nearly triple coal output in April
stories.summary: US renewable energy generation hit a significant milestone in April, with total renewables — including small-scale rooftop solar — generating nearly three times the electricity output of coal. The milestone is notable because it represents a crossover point that was projected but has now been confirmed with actual generation data. Small-scale solar is an important part of this story: residential and commercial rooftop installations, which don't show up in traditional utility-scale generation statistics, pushed the numbers significantly higher than grid-connected generation alone. The 'not on the grid' caveat in the article title is important: some of this solar generation is consumed locally and never flows through the transmission grid, which means grid operators still have to plan for coal capacity to cover demand that solar can't reliably meet. Coal's decline has been dramatic over the past decade, driven by a combination of cheap natural gas, falling renewable costs, and policy pressures. The April timing matters too — spring is the best month for solar generation in most of the US, with long days and mild temperatures that don't drive high air conditioning load, so April numbers represent a favorable scenario rather than average performance. Still, the trend line is clear and directional: coal's share of US electricity generation has been falling consistently, and renewables have been filling the gap. For data centers and AI infrastructure operators thinking about their power procurement strategies, the expanding renewable capacity is relevant context.
